summary: I was born in Tokyo, although I moved around so much that I don't really know exactly where. Tokyo is where my life began before I eventually came to the United States in 1917. Even though I left at a young age, Tokyo remains the place of my origin.

Mrs. Fukuda was born in Kumamoto-ken, which is where her roots lie. She mentioned this when we talked about our backgrounds and families in Japan. Kumamoto Prefecture is important because it connects to her early life before coming to the United States.

I came to the United States in 1917, following my father who had already settled here. Life was tough at first, especially with the Alien Land Law in effect since 1913, which prevented Japanese people from owning or leasing land. I worked various jobs, including as a houseboy in Walnut Creek and San Francisco, and attended English school at Pine Methodist Church. The United States became my home for over 52 years, though I faced many challenges as an immigrant.
cues: work in Walnut Creek and San Francisco

summary: When I arrived in the United States in 1917, the Alien Land Law had been in effect since 1913. This law prevented Japanese immigrants like my father and me from buying or even leasing land, which made it very difficult to establish a stable livelihood. My father worked as a share cropper in Oakland because of this restriction, raising tomatoes and cucumbers on land he did not own.

My father worked as a share cropper in Oakland, raising tomatoes and cucumbers on a 50-50 basis with landlords. Oakland was one of the places where we lived and worked, though he had to move around depending on job availability. It was a tough life, and some years were better than others, but Oakland was a key location in our early years in America.

My father initially tried to start a business selling canned goods and pickles from Japan in the Hayward area. Unfortunately, the business did not work out well, so he had to quit and later became a share cropper. Hayward was the first place where my father attempted to establish himself in the United States.

When I first came to the United States, I worked as a houseboy in Walnut Creek and San Francisco. My duties included making breakfast, cleaning the kitchen, and doing various chores after school. Walnut Creek was one of the places where I experienced the hard work and discipline of immigrant life in those early years.

San Francisco was where I attended English school for Japanese people at the Pine Methodist Church. I also worked as a houseboy there and later returned several times to continue my education and work. It was a central place in my life as I balanced schooling and labor during my youth in America.

I went to an English school for Japanese people located at the Pine United Methodist Church in San Francisco. This church was more than a place of worship; it was a community center where I learned English and connected with others. My schooling there was part of my effort to adapt and succeed in the United States.

In 1922, I came to the United States with my parents, but we were classified as Hi-Imin, or non-immigrants. This classification affected our legal status and opportunities. Despite this, I helped with my parents' business and continued to work hard to build a life here.

At one point, we lived in Wyoming before moving back to Sacramento. It was during this time that I got married in 1926. Wyoming was part of our journey as we searched for stability and community in the United States.

Sacramento became our home after we moved back from Wyoming, where my brother worked for Aki Shoten, a well-known store. I settled here with my family and went back to school to improve my English. I was also a member of the Presbyterian Church in Sacramento, which connected me to the community and to my future wife.

My brother used to work for Aki Shoten, a store well known among the older Japanese community in Sacramento. Because of his connection there, we decided to settle down in Sacramento. Aki Shoten was an important part of our family's ties to the local Japanese American community.

During summer vacations, I worked in a cannery in Monterey to earn money for school. This was a common practice among students like me who needed to support their education. Monterey was one of the places where I gained work experience and saved money for my studies.

I spent some time in San Diego working in a nursery owned by Japanese people. Even though I was 19 years old, my work there was similar to that of a schoolboy. When the owner sold the property and returned to Japan, I had to leave and go back to San Francisco.

I also worked for a nurseryman in San Jose during the summer. This was part of my ongoing effort to earn money and support myself while continuing my education. San Jose was one of several California cities where I worked before settling down.

I was a member of the Presbyterian Church in Sacramento, which played a significant role in my social life and faith. It was through this church that I met my future wife, who belonged to the Baptist Church but attended meetings with me. The church community also connected me with important people like Rev. D. Toriumi and others who supported us.

My future wife was a member of the Baptist Church, and although I belonged to the Presbyterian Church, we met through church meetings where we both participated. This connection helped our relationship grow and eventually led to our marriage. The Baptist Church was part of her community and faith background.

I belonged to the Fujinkai, a group where older and younger women gathered to make tea for people attending meetings. Although it was not a large organization, it was a way for me to be involved in the community and support church activities. Fujinkai helped me connect with others and participate in social events.

I knew a man who lived in the lower area of the Sacramento River, and this connection influenced my decision to come to Sacramento. The river area was part of the community where I sought to improve my English and continue my education. It was a stepping stone in my life after working as a share cropper in Oakland.

I was advised by a man named Tagawa in San Lorenzo to look up Rev. Nishimura if I wanted to go to Los Angeles. When I found Rev. Nishimura, I stayed at his house, which had a chapel on the first floor and a boarding house on the second. Los Angeles was where I began to learn about faith and spirituality under Rev. Nishimura's guidance.

Rev. Nishimura was a key figure I met in Los Angeles when I was looking for guidance. His house served as a chapel and boarding house for many people, and I stayed there to learn about faith. He influenced my spiritual journey and helped me during a transitional period in my life.

Rev. D. Toriumi and his parents were very close to my family and acted like my own parents. Mrs. Toriumi was my sewing teacher, and I almost lived with them. Through these connections, discussions about my marriage advanced, showing how important Rev. Toriumi was in my personal life.
